summary: The author achieved an 8th place finish on a Jane Street real-time market data forecasting competition by applying deep learning to tabular time-series data. They used a time-series GRU architecture with simple standardization and zero-imputation, augmented with market averages, rolling statistics, and auxiliary responder targets. A key innovation was online learning during inference, updating model weights with a single forward pass per date, which significantly boosted performance. The solution also prioritized inference speed optimization and used a simple unweighted ensemble of multiple seeds.
extensive_summary:
## Cross-Validation & Data Preparation
The author employed a time-series cross-validation scheme with two folds, using a validation size of 200 dates to mirror the public leaderboard setup. To better simulate the private dataset scenario, the first fold's model was tested on the last 200 dates with a 200-day gap. Data preparation involved starting from `date_id = 700` (where `time_id` count stabilizes) and applying simple standardization with zero imputation for NaNs, as more complex methods yielded no gains.

## Feature Engineering & Model Architecture
All original features were used except three categorical ones (09–11). The author engineered additional features including market averages per `date_id` and `time_id`, rolling statistics (averages and standard deviations over the last 1000 `time_id`s per symbol), and explicitly added `time_id` as a feature. The core architecture consisted of two slightly different time-series GRU models: a 3-layer GRU and a 1-layer GRU followed by two linear layers with ReLU and dropout. The latter performed slightly better on CV but both were kept for ensembling.

## Auxiliary Targets & Training Strategy
A crucial component was the use of four auxiliary "responder" targets (`responder_7`, `responder_8`, and two calculated ones approximating 8-day and 60-day rolling averages of the base target). Separate base models were trained for each auxiliary target, with their predictions passed through a linear layer to produce the final `responder_6` output. The sum of weighted zero-mean R² losses across all responders was used for training. Models were trained with a batch size of one day and a learning rate of 0.0005.

## Online Learning & Inference Optimization
During inference, the author implemented online learning by performing a single forward pass per `date_id` to update model weights with a learning rate of 0.0003, using only the `responder_6` loss. This dramatically improved CV scores (+0.008) and proved highly effective over the year-long gap between training and private data. Inference speed was heavily optimized, achieving 0.06 seconds per step, with PyTorch chosen over TensorFlow after benchmarking. The final submission used a simple unweighted average of predictions from 6 models (2 architectures × 3 seeds).
competition_flow: Raw tabular time-series data was standardized and zero-imputed, augmented with rolling statistics and market averages, fed into multiple GRU architectures trained with auxiliary responder targets and weighted R² loss, then optimized for inference speed and updated via single-pass online learning during the competition period before being ensembled via unweighted averaging.
features_engineering: Rolling averages and standard deviations over the last 1000 `time_id`s per symbol
Auxiliary responder targets (`responder_9`, `responder_10`) calculated via shifted `responder_6` and `responder_8` columns
models: 1-layer GRU followed by 2 linear layers with ReLU and dropout
cv_strategy: Time-series cross-validation with two folds (200 dates each), plus a 200-day gap holdout on the final 200 dates to simulate the private leaderboard scenario.
ensembling: Simple unweighted average of predictions from 6 models (2 GRU architectures × 3 random seeds).
insights: Starting from `date_id = 700` stabilizes the data without hurting performance.
Simple standardization and zero imputation are sufficient; complex preprocessing yields no gains.
Auxiliary rolling-average targets significantly improve both CV and LB scores.
Online learning via single-pass weight updates during inference is highly effective for bridging training-to-inference distribution shifts.
Inference speed optimization is critical for real-time tabular forecasting competitions.
critical_findings: For an MLP model, the score without online learning was higher than for the GRU, but lower with online learning, indicating GRUs adapt better to incremental updates.
Performing one-day online updates for almost a year is enough to maintain strong performance, making full retraining unnecessary despite the large data gap.
what_did_not_work: MLP, time-series transformers, cross-symbol attention, and embeddings
Using the entire dataset instead of filtering from `date_id = 700`
